An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a “one-degree angle,” and can be used to measure angles.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: An angle that turns through *n* one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of *n* degrees.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions (e.g., by using a visual fraction model). *Examples: 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 ;3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8 ; 2 1/8 = 1 + 1 + 1/8 = 8/8 + 8/8 + 1/8.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators (e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators (e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b. *For example, use a visual fraction model to represent 5/4 as the product 5 × (1/4), recording the conclusion by the equation 5/4 = 5 × (1/4).*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number. *For example, use a visual fraction model to express 3 × (2/5) as 6 × (1/5), recognizing this product as 6/5. (In general, n × (a/b) = (n × a)/b.)*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number (e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem). Check for the reasonableness of the answer. *For example, if each person at a party will eat 3/8 of a pound of roast beef, and there will be 5 people at the party, how many pounds of roast beef will be needed? Between what two whole numbers does your answer lie?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Read and write decimals to thousandths using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form [e.g., 347.392 = 3 x 100 + 4 x 10 + 7 x 1 + 3 (1/10) + 9 (1/100) + 2 (1/1000)].
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compare two decimals to thousandths place based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: A cube with side length 1 unit, called a “unit cube,” is said to have “one cubic unit” of volume, and can be used to measure volume.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: A solid figure that can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes is said to have a volume of *n* cubic units.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Estimate and measure volumes by counting unit cubes, using cubic cm, cubic in, cubic ft, and non-standard units.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description:
Estimate and find the volume of a right rectangular prism with whole-number side lengths by packing it with unit cubes, and show that the volume is the same as would be found by multiplying the edge lengths, equivalently by multiplying the height by the area of the base. 
Demonstrate the associative property of multiplication by using the product of three whole-numbers to find volumes (length x width x height).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Apply the formulas *V = l × w × h* and *V = b × h* for rectangular prisms to find volumes of right rectangular prisms with whole number edge lengths in the context of solving real world and mathematical problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize volume as additive. Find volumes of solid figures composed of two, non-overlapping, right rectangular prisms by adding the volumes of the non-overlapping parts, applying this technique to solve real world problems.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret the product (*a/b*) × *q* as a parts of a partition of q into b equal parts; equivalently, as the result of a sequence of operations *a* × *q* ÷ *b*. *For example, use a visual fraction model to show (2/3) × 4 = 8/3, and create a story context for this equation. Do the same with (2/3) × (4/5) = 8/15. (In general, (a/b) × (c/d) = ac/bd.)*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Comparing the size of a product to the size of one factor on the basis of the size of the other factor, without performing the indicated multiplication.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction greater than 1 results in a product greater than the given number (recognizing multiplication by whole numbers greater than 1 as a familiar case); explaining why multiplying a given number by a fraction less than 1 results in a product smaller than the given number; and relating the principle of fraction equivalence *a/b* = (*n×a*)/(*n×b*) to the effect of multiplying a/b by 1. (Division of a fraction by a fraction is not a requirement at this grade.)
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret division of a unit fraction by a non-zero whole number, and compute such quotients. *For example, create a story context for (1/3) ÷ 4, and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that (1/3) ÷ 4 = 1/12 because (1/12) × 4 = 1/3.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret division of a whole number by a unit fraction, and compute such quotients. *For example, create a story context for 4 ÷ (1/5), and use a visual fraction model to show the quotient. Use the relationship between multiplication and division to explain that 4 ÷ (1/5) = 20 because 20 × (1/5) = 4.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve real world problems involving division of unit fractions by non-zero whole numbers and division of whole numbers by unit fractions (e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem). *For example, how much chocolate will each person get if 3 people share 1/2 lb of chocolate equally? How many 1/3-cup servings are in 2 cups of raisins?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Make tables of equivalent ratios relating quantities with whole number measurements, find missing values in the tables, and plot the pairs of values on the coordinate plane. Use tables to compare ratios, and understand equivalencies.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve unit rate problems including those involving unit pricing and constant speed. *For example, if it took 7 hours to mow 4 lawns, then at that rate, how many lawns could be mowed in 35 hours? At what rate were lawns being mowed?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 (e.g., 30% of a quantity means 30/100 times the quantity); solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use ratio reasoning to convert measurement units between given measurement systems (e.g., convert kilometers to miles); manipulate and transform units appropriately when multiplying or dividing quantities.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize opposite signs of numbers as indicating locations on opposite sides of 0 on the number line; Recognize that the opposite of the opposite of a number is the number itself [e.g., –(–3) = 3] and that 0 is its own opposite.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand signs of numbers in ordered pairs as indicating locations in quadrants of the coordinate plane; recognize that when two ordered pairs differ only by signs, the locations of the points are related by reflections across one or both axes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find and position integers and other rational numbers on a horizontal or vertical number line diagram; find and position pairs of integers and other rational numbers on a coordinate plane.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret statements of inequality as statements about the relative position of two numbers on a number line diagram. *For example, interpret –3 > –7 as a statement that –3 is located to the right of –7 on a number line oriented from left to right.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Write, interpret, and explain statements of order for rational numbers in real-world contexts. For example, write –3° C > –7° C to express the fact that –3° C is warmer than –7° C.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand the absolute value of a rational number as its distance from 0 on the number line; interpret absolute value as magnitude for a positive or negative quantity in a real-world situation. *For example, for an account balance of –30 dollars, write |–30| = 30 to describe the size of the debt in dollars.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Distinguish comparisons of absolute value from statements about order. *For example, recognize that an account balance less than -30 dollars represents a debt greater than 30 dollars.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Write expressions that record operations with numbers and with letters standing for numbers. *For example, express the calculation “Subtract y from 5” as 5 – y.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify parts of an expression using mathematical terms (sum, term, product, factor, quotient, coefficient); view one or more parts of an expression as a single entity. *For example, describe the expression 2 (8 + 7) as a product of two factors; view (8 + 7) as both a single entity and a sum of two terms.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Evaluate expressions and formulas. Include formulas used in real-world problems. Perform arithmetic operations, including those involving whole number exponents, in the conventional order with or without parentheses. (Order of Operations)
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Reporting the number of observations (occurrences).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Describing the nature of the attribute under investigation, including how it was measured and its units of measurement.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Giving quantitative measures of center (median and/or mean) and variability (interquartile range), as well as describing any overall pattern and any outliers with reference to the context in which the data were gathered.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Relating the choice of measures of center and variability to the shape of the data distribution and the context in which the data were gathered.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship (e.g., by testing for equivalent ratios in a table or graphing on a coordinate plane and observing whether the graph is a straight line through the origin).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Identify the constant of proportionality (unit rate) in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions of proportional relationships in real world situations.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent proportional relationships by equations and multiple representations such as tables, graphs, diagrams, sequences, and contextual situations. *For example, if total cost t is proportional to the number n of items purchased at a constant price p, the relationship between the total cost and the number of items can be expressed as t = pn.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand the concept of unit rate and show it on a coordinate plane. Explain what a point (x, y) on the graph of a proportional relationship means in terms of the situation, with special attention to the points (0, 0) and (1, r) where r is the unit rate.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that a number and its opposite have a sum of 0 (additive inverses). Describe situations in which opposite quantities combine to make 0. *For example, a hydrogen atom has 0 charge because its two constituents are oppositely charged.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand addition of rational numbers (p + q as the number located a distance |q| from p, in the positive or negative direction depending on whether q is positive or negative). Interpret sums of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand subtraction of rational numbers as adding the additive inverse, p – q = p + (–q). Show that the distance between two rational numbers on the number line is the absolute value of their difference, and apply this principle in real-world contexts.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to add and subtract rational numbers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand that multiplication is extended from fractions to rational numbers by requiring that operations continue to satisfy the properties of operations, particularly the distributive property, leading to products such as (–1)(–1) = 1 and the rules for multiplying signed numbers. Interpret products of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand that integers can be divided, provided that the divisor is not zero, and every quotient of integers (with non-zero divisor) is a rational number. If p and q are integers, then –(p/q) = (–p)/q = p/(–q). Interpret quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Apply and name properties of operations used as strategies to multiply and divide rational numbers.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Convert a rational number to a decimal using long division; know that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Convert between equivalent fractions, decimals, or percents.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. *For example, the perimeter of a rectangle is 54 cm. Its length is 6 cm. What is its width?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve word problems leading to inequalities of the form px + q > r or px + q < r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Graph the solution set of the inequality and interpret it in the context of the problem. *For example: As a salesperson, you are paid $50 per week plus $3 per sale. This week you want your pay to be at least $100. Write an inequality for the number of sales you need to make, and describe the solutions.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Design a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes, and use the model to determine probabilities of events. *For example, if a student is selected at random from a class, find the probability that Jane will be selected and the probability that a girl will be selected.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Design a probability model (which may not be uniform) by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process. *For example, find the approximate probability that a spinning penny will land heads up or that a tossed paper cup will land open-end down. Do the outcomes for the spinning penny appear to be equally likely based on the observed frequencies?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand that, just as with simple events, the probability of a compound event is the fraction of outcomes in the sample space for which the compound event occurs.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables and tree diagrams. For an event described in everyday language (e.g., “rolling double sixes”), identify the outcomes in the sample space which compose the event.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Design and use a simulation to generate frequencies for compound events. *For example, use random digits as a simulation tool to approximate the answer to the question: If 40% of donors have type A blood, what is the probability that it will take at least 4 donors to find one with type A blood?*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Lines are taken to lines, and line segments to line segments of the same length.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Angles are taken to angles of the same measure.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Parallel lines are taken to parallel lines.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Give examples of linear equations in one variable with one solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solutions. Show which of these possibilities is the case by successively transforming the given equation into simpler forms, until an equivalent equation of the form *x = a, a = a, or a = b* results (where *a* and *b* are different numbers).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve linear equations with rational coefficients, including equations whose solutions require expanding expressions using the distributive property and combining like terms.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that the solution to a system of two linear equations in two variables is the intersection of the graphs of those equations because points of intersection satisfy both equations simultaneously.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve systems of two linear equations in two variables and estimate solutions by graphing the equations. Simple cases may be done by inspection. *For example, 3x + 2y = 5 and  3x + 2y = 6 have no solution because 3x + 2y cannot simultaneously be 5 and 6.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems leading to two linear equations in two variables. *For example, given coordinates for two pairs of points, determine whether the line through the first pair of points intersects the line through the second pair.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Add vectors end-to-end, component-wise, and by the parallelogram rule. Understand that the magnitude of a sum of two vectors is typically not the sum of the magnitudes.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Given two vectors in magnitude and direction form, determine the magnitude and direction of their sum.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Understand vector subtraction v – w as v + (–w), where –w is the additive inverse of w, with the same magnitude as w and pointing in the opposite direction. Represent vector subtraction graphically by connecting the tips in the appropriate order, and perform vector subtraction component-wise.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Represent scalar multiplication graphically by scaling vectors and possibly reversing their direction; perform scalar multiplication component-wise, e.g., as c(v<sub>x</sub>, v<sub>y</sub>) = (cv<sub>x</sub>, cv<sub>y</sub>).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Compute the magnitude of a scalar multiple cv using ||cv|| = |c|v. Compute the direction of cv knowing that when |c|v ≠ 0, the direction of cv is either along v (for c > 0) or against v (for c < 0).
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret parts of an expression, such as terms, factors, and coefficients.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Interpret complicated expressions by viewing one or more of their parts as a single entity. *For example, interpret P(1+r)<sup>n</sup> as the product of P and a factor not depending on P.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Factor a quadratic expression to reveal the zeros of the function it defines. *For example, x² + 4x +3 = (x +3)(x +1).*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Complete the square in a quadratic expression to reveal the maximum or minimum value of the function it defines. *For example, x² + 4x + 3 = (x + 2)² - 1.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the properties of exponents to transform expressions for exponential functions. *For example the expression 1.15t can be rewritten as (1.15<sup>1/12</sup>)<sup>12t</sup> ≈ 1.012<sup>12t</sup> to reveal the approximate equivalent monthly interest rate if the annual rate is 15%.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the method of completing the square to transform any quadratic equation in x into an equation of the form (x – p)² = *q* that has the same solutions. Derive the quadratic formula from this form.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve quadratic equations by inspection (e.g., for *x*² = 49), taking square roots, completing the square, the quadratic formula and factoring, as appropriate to the initial form of the equation. Recognize when the quadratic formula gives complex solutions and write them as *a* ± *bi* for real numbers *a* and *b*.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph linear and quadratic functions and show intercepts, maxima, and minima.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph square root, cube root, and piecewise-defined functions, including step functions and absolute value functions.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph polynomial functions, identifying zeros (using technology) or algebraic methods when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: (+) Graph rational functions, identifying zeros and discontinuities (asymptotes/holes) using technology, and algebraic methods when suitable factorizations are available, and showing end behavior.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Graph exponential and logarithmic functions, showing intercepts and end behavior, and trigonometric functions, showing period, midline, and amplitude.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the process of factoring and completing the square in a quadratic function to show zeros, extreme values, and symmetry of the graph, and interpret these in terms of a context.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Use the properties of exponents to interpret expressions for exponential functions. *For example, identify percent rate of change in functions such as y = (1.02)<sup>t</sup>, y = (0.97)<sup>t</sup>, y = (1.01)<sup>12t</sup>, y = (1.2)<sup>t/10</sup>, and classify them as representing exponential growth or decay.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Determine an explicit expression, a recursive process, or steps for calculation from a context.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Combine standard function types using arithmetic operations. *For example, build a function that models the temperature of a cooling body by adding a constant function to a decaying exponential, and relate these functions to the model.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: (+) Compose functions. *For example, if T(y) is the temperature in the atmosphere as a function of height, and h(t) is the height of a weather balloon as a function of time, then T(h(t)) is the temperature at the location of the weather balloon as a function of time.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Solve an equation of the form *f(x) = c* for a simple function *f* that has an inverse and write an expression for the inverse. *For example, f(x) = 2x3 for x > 0 or f(x) = (x + 1)/(x – 1) for x ≠ 1.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: (+) Verify by composition that one function is the inverse of another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: (+) Read values of an inverse function from a graph or a table, given that the function has an inverse.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: (+) Produce an invertible function from a non-invertible function by restricting the domain.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Show that linear functions grow by equal differences over equal intervals, and that exponential functions grow by equal factors over equal intervals.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize situations in which one quantity changes at a constant rate per unit interval relative to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Recognize situations in which a quantity grows or decays by a constant percent rate per unit interval relative to another.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: A dilation takes a line not passing through the center of the dilation to a parallel line, and leaves a line passing through the center unchanged.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: The dilation of a line segment is longer or shorter in the ratio given by the scale factor.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Fit a function to the data; use functions fitted to data to solve problems in the context of the data. *Use given functions or choose a function suggested by the context. Emphasize linear, quadratic, and exponential models.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Informally assess the fit of a function by plotting and analyzing residuals. *For example: Describe solutions to problems that require interpolation and extrapolation.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Fit a linear function for a scatter plot that suggests a linear association.
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Find the expected payoff for a game of chance. *For example, find the expected winnings from a state lottery ticket or a game at a fast-food restaurant.*
properties.author: 1EdTech

properties.description: Evaluate and compare strategies on the basis of expected values. *For example, compare a high-deductible versus a low-deductible automobile insurance policy using various, but reasonable, chances of having a minor or a major accident.*
properties.author: 1EdTech
